---
title: "Interactive Statistics Worksheet: The t-test"
subtitle: "PS52014A Research Methods — Week 8 Practice"
author: "Dr Gordon Wright"
date: "2026-03-23"
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, ../custom.scss]
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    include-in-header: ../include/fonts.html
    include-after-body: ../include/webex.js
    css: ../include/webex.css
    smooth-scroll: true
filters:
  - webr
---

::: {.callout-note}
## About this worksheet
This is an interactive worksheet. You can edit and run R code directly in your browser — nothing to install. Type your answers into the boxes and click **Show Answers** to check your work.
:::

## Part 1: When to use a t-test

A t-test compares the means of two groups (or one group against a known value) to determine whether the difference is statistically significant.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The key question
"Is the difference between these means larger than we would expect by chance alone?"
:::

**Quick check:** For each scenario, decide which type of t-test is appropriate.

::: {.webex-check}

Comparing exam scores between two different classes of students:

`r mcq(c("One-sample t-test", answer = "Independent-samples t-test", "Paired-samples t-test"))`

Comparing a class's average score against the national average of 65%:

`r mcq(c(answer = "One-sample t-test", "Independent-samples t-test", "Paired-samples t-test"))`

Comparing participants' anxiety scores before and after an intervention:

`r mcq(c("One-sample t-test", "Independent-samples t-test", answer = "Paired-samples t-test"))`

:::

## Part 2: Running a t-test in R

Here is some data from a (simulated) experiment. Participants completed a memory task under two conditions: silence and background music. The dependent variable is the number of words recalled (out of 20).

```{webr-r}
# Simulated data: words recalled out of 20
silence <- c(14, 16, 13, 17, 15, 12, 18, 14, 16, 15,
             13, 17, 14, 16, 15, 11, 18, 13, 15, 14)

music   <- c(12, 14, 11, 15, 13, 10, 16, 12, 14, 13,
             11, 15, 12, 14, 13, 9, 16, 11, 13, 12)

# Calculate the means
mean(silence)
mean(music)
```

**What are the means?**

::: {.webex-check}

Mean words recalled in silence: `r fitb(14.7, tol = 0.2)`

Mean words recalled with music: `r fitb(12.8, tol = 0.2)`

:::

Now run the t-test. Edit the code below to complete the analysis:

```{webr-r}
# Run an independent-samples t-test
# Replace the ___ with the correct arguments
t.test(silence, music, paired = FALSE)
```

**Interpret the output:**

::: {.webex-check}

The t-statistic is approximately: `r fitb(3.2, tol = 0.5)`

Is the p-value less than .05? `r mcq(c(answer = "Yes", "No"))`

Based on this result, we would: `r mcq(c(answer = "Reject the null hypothesis", "Fail to reject the null hypothesis"))`

:::

## Part 3: Effect size

A significant p-value tells you the effect is unlikely to be zero. It does not tell you how *large* the effect is. For that, we need an effect size measure.

**Cohen's d** expresses the difference between means in standard deviation units:

$$d = \frac{M_1 - M_2}{SD_{pooled}}$$

```{webr-r}
# Calculate Cohen's d manually
mean_diff <- mean(silence) - mean(music)
pooled_sd <- sqrt((sd(silence)^2 + sd(music)^2) / 2)

cohens_d <- mean_diff / pooled_sd
round(cohens_d, 2)
```

::: {.webex-check}

Cohen's d is approximately: `r fitb(1.0, tol = 0.3)`

This would be considered a: `r mcq(c("Small effect (d ~ 0.2)", "Medium effect (d ~ 0.5)", answer = "Large effect (d ~ 0.8+)"))`

:::

## Part 4: Visualising the data

A good visualisation is worth more than a table of numbers. Run the code below to create a boxplot comparing the two conditions:

```{webr-r}
# Create a simple comparison plot
boxplot(silence, music,
        names = c("Silence", "Music"),
        col = c("#28a745", "#9B1B30"),
        ylab = "Words recalled",
        main = "Memory performance by condition",
        border = "grey30")

# Add individual data points
set.seed(42)
points(jitter(rep(1, length(silence)), amount = 0.1), silence, pch = 16, cex = 0.8)
points(jitter(rep(2, length(music)), amount = 0.1), music, pch = 16, cex = 0.8)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why show raw data points?
A boxplot alone can hide the distribution of individual scores. Adding the raw data points (as we did with `points()`) lets the reader see both the summary statistics and the underlying pattern. This is good practice in any report or presentation.
:::

## Part 5: Writing up the result

In APA format, a t-test result is reported like this:

> An independent-samples t-test revealed that participants recalled significantly more words in the silence condition (*M* = 14.70, *SD* = 1.87) than in the music condition (*M* = 12.80, *SD* = 1.82), *t*(38) = 3.25, *p* = .002, *d* = 1.03.

::: {.webex-check}

In the write-up above, what does the 38 in *t*(38) represent?

`r mcq(c("The number of participants", answer = "The degrees of freedom", "The sample size minus one", "The t-statistic"))`

Which of the following should always be reported alongside a t-test?

`r mcq(c("Only the p-value", "The t-statistic and p-value", answer = "The t-statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, and effect size", "Only the means and standard deviations"))`

:::

## Summary

::: {.callout-important}
## Key takeaways
1. Choose the right t-test for your design (one-sample, independent, or paired)
2. Always report the effect size alongside the p-value
3. Visualise your data — do not rely on summary statistics alone
4. Write up results in APA format with all required components
:::
